title: Strong vs Weak Technologies Framework
description: Chris Dixon's foundational framework distinguishing technology pairs that reshape vs adapt to the world

lessons.title: Strong vs Weak Technologies
lessons.content:
## The iPhone Moment

When the iPhone launched in 2007, critics dismissed it. The wireless network was too slow. Users were accustomed to physical keyboards. There were no apps. Calls dropped frequently. Yet Steve Jobs smiled when a journalist complained about the touchscreen keyboard and simply said: **'Your thumbs will learn.'**

The iPhone was so compelling that the world adapted to it вҖ” carriers built out wireless capacity, developers created new apps, users learned touchscreen typing. This is the essence of **strong technology**.

## The Pattern

Technologies usually emerge in pairs: a **strong form** and a **weak form**.

| Strong (reshapes world) | Weak (adapts to world) |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| Public internet | Private intranets |
| iPhone (full OS) | Blackberry (keyboard) |
| Permissionless blockchain | Private blockchain |
| Full electric vehicles | Hybrid cars |
| VR | AR |

## Why Weak Tech Feels Safer

Weak technologies appear more practical to incumbents. They're familiar, productive, serious, safe. Strong technologies look strange, toy-like, unserious, expensive, even dangerous. So organizations choose the weak compromise.

## The Long Game

Strong technologies captivate enthusiasts вҖ” weekend hobbyists, not paid employees. Enthusiasts think long-term, design from first principles, and vote with their time. When the mainstream world finally notices, it's often too late to catch up.

**Your thumbs will learn.** The world adapts to strong technologies, not the other way around.
key_ideas: Technologies emerge in pairs: strong (revolutionary) and weak (compromise)
Strong tech reshapes the world; weak tech adapts to it
Weak tech feels safer but strong tech defines new eras
Enthusiasts recognize strong tech early and vote with their time
iPhone made carriers, developers, and users change вҖ” not vice versa
lessons.exercise:
Spotify(мҠӨнҠёлҰ¬л°Қ мқҢм•…)мҷҖ iTunes(MP3 лӢӨмҡҙлЎңл“ң) мӨ‘ Strong TechnologyлҠ” м–ҙлҠҗ мӘҪмқёк°Җ?

1) Spotify вҖ” it required the world to adapt (always-on internet, subscription model)
2) iTunes вҖ” лЁјм Җ м¶ңмӢңлҗҳм–ҙ мҙҲкё° мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗ кё°л°ҳмқҙ лҚ” м»ёмңјлҜҖлЎң мӢңмһҘмқ„ м„ м җн•ң кё°мҲ 
3) л‘җ кё°мҲ  лӘЁл‘җ кё°мЎҙ мқҢм•… мҶҢл№„ л°©мӢқм—җ м Ғмқ‘н•ң Weak Technologyм—җ н•ҙлӢ№
4) SpotifyлҠ” Strongмқҙм§Җл§Ң iTunesлҸ„ л””м§Җн„ё мқҢмӣҗ мң нҶөмқ„ к°•м ңн–ҲмңјлҜҖлЎң лҸҷмқј лІ”мЈј
lessons.answer: Spotify вҖ” it required the world to adapt (always-on internet, subscription model)
lessons.explanation: iTunes was the weak compromise вҖ” it fit the existing mental model of owning files. Spotify was strong: it required always-on internet (not guaranteed in 2008), subscription payments (people wanted to 'own'), and trusting your music to the cloud. Spotify made the world adapt, and now streaming dominates.

lessons.title: The Hype Cycle and Trough of Disillusionment
lessons.content:
## The Five Phases

Strong technologies often follow the Perez/Gartner hype cycle:

```
Innovation вҶ’ Peak of Inflated вҶ’ Trough of вҶ’ Slope of вҶ’ Plateau of
Trigger      Expectations      Disillusionment   Enlightenment   Productivity
```

## The Critical Moment: The Trough

The **trough of disillusionment** is where strong technologies are tested. After the initial hype fades, reality sets in:
- The technology doesn't work as promised yet
- Mainstream adoption seems distant
- Investors lose patience
- Critics say 'I told you so'

This is when entrepreneurs who bet on strong tech often lose faith and pivot to weak tech. **This is usually a mistake.**

## Why Weak Tech Looks Better in the Trough

Weak technologies appear closer to mainstream adoption because they don't require the world to change. A Blackberry with a better keyboard seems more achievable than convincing everyone to learn touchscreen typing.

But weak tech rarely defines the future.

## Historical Examples

**Public Internet (Strong)** went through the trough in the early 1990s. Companies built private intranets instead (weak). By 2000, the public internet won.

**Blockchain (Strong)** hit the trough in 2018-2019. Enterprises built private blockchains (weak). Public blockchains survived and define the space today.

## The Lesson

If you're building strong tech and hit the trough, don't pivot to weak tech. The trough is not proof of failure вҖ” it's a phase. Strong technologies that survive the trough become the technologies that define eras.
key_ideas: Hype cycle: trigger вҶ’ peak вҶ’ trough вҶ’ slope вҶ’ plateau
Trough of disillusionment tests strong technology believers
Entrepreneurs often pivot from strong to weak tech in the trough
Weak tech seems closer to adoption but rarely defines the future
Strong tech that survives the trough reshapes the world
lessons.exercise:
лё”лЎқмІҙмқёмқҙ 2018-2019л…„ 'нҷҳл©ёмқҳ кіЁм§ңкё°(Trough of Disillusionment)'м—җ л№ мЎҢмқ„ л•Ң, л§ҺмқҖ кё°м—…мқҙ м„ нғқн•ң Weak Technology м „нҷҳмқҖ?

1) Built private/permissioned blockchains
2) нҚјлё”лҰӯ лё”лЎқмІҙмқёмқҳ н•©мқҳ л©”м»ӨлӢҲмҰҳмқ„ PoSлЎң м „нҷҳн•ҳм—¬ нҡЁмңЁм„ұмқ„ к°ңм„ 
3) лё”лЎқмІҙмқё кё°мҲ мқ„ нҸ¬кё°н•ҳкі  кё°мЎҙ мӨ‘м•ҷнҷ” лҚ°мқҙн„°лІ мқҙмҠӨлЎң нҡҢк·Җ
4) нғҲмӨ‘м•ҷнҷ” мӣҗм№ҷмқ„ мң м§Җн•ҳл©ҙм„ң Layer 2 нҷ•мһҘм„ұ мҶ”лЈЁм…ҳ к°ңл°ңм—җ м§‘мӨ‘
lessons.explanation: Private blockchains were the weak technology compromise вҖ” they kept the blockchain label but removed what made public blockchains powerful (permissionless, censorship-resistant, credibly neutral). Enterprises chose them because they felt safer and more controllable, but they failed to capture blockchain's real value.

lessons.title: The Unreasonable Man
lessons.content:
## George Bernard Shaw's Insight

> 'The reasonable man adapts himself to the world: the unreasonable one persists in trying to adapt the world to himself. Therefore all progress depends on the unreasonable man.'

This is the philosophical core of strong vs weak technologies.

## Two Approaches to Innovation

| Approach | Philosophy | Example |
|----------|-----------|--------|
| **Reasonable (Weak)** | Adapt to current world | Blackberry kept keyboards because users knew them |
| **Unreasonable (Strong)** | Make world adapt | iPhone removed keyboard, said 'your thumbs will learn' |

## Why Strong Tech Requires Unreasonableness

Strong technologies demand changes:
- iPhone: Carriers had to build out wireless networks
- iPhone: Developers had to learn new paradigms
- iPhone: Users had to relearn typing

A 'reasonable' person would say: 'This is too much to ask. Just make a better keyboard phone.' That's the weak tech path.

## The Entrepreneurial Choice

When you're in the trough of disillusionment:
- **Reasonable:** Pivot to weak tech that fits the current world
- **Unreasonable:** Persist in making the world adapt

## Progress Requires Unreasonableness

Every transformative technology required unreasonable persistence:
- **Tesla:** Electric cars when everyone said they'd never work
- **Bitcoin:** Digital currency when everyone said it needed government backing
- **SpaceX:** Reusable rockets when NASA said it was impossible

The reasonable people adapt. The unreasonable people shape the future.
key_ideas: Reasonable people adapt to the world; unreasonable adapt world to themselves
All progress depends on the unreasonable
Weak tech is 'reasonable' вҖ” fits current world
Strong tech is 'unreasonable' вҖ” demands world change
Transformative innovation requires unreasonable persistence
lessons.exercise:
TeslaлҠ” м—…кі„к°Җ н•ҳмқҙлёҢлҰ¬л“ңк°Җ лҚ” нҳ„мӢӨм Ғмқҙлқјкі  мЈјмһҘн•  л•Ң мҷ„м „ м „кё°м°ЁлҘј кі мҲҳн–ҲлӢӨ. Teslaк°Җ м·Ён•ң м ‘к·јлІ•мқҖ?

1) Unreasonable вҖ” made the world adapt to their vision
2) Reasonable вҖ” мӢңмһҘмқҙ мӣҗн•ҳлҠ” н•ҳмқҙлёҢлҰ¬л“ңмҷҖ м „кё°м°ЁлҘј лҸҷмӢңм—җ к°ңл°ңн•ҳм—¬ лҰ¬мҠӨнҒ¬лҘј л¶„мӮ°
3) Weak Technology м ‘к·ј вҖ” кё°мЎҙ мһҗлҸҷм°Ё мқён”„лқјм—җ м „кё°м°ЁлҘј м җм§„м ҒмңјлЎң нҶөн•©
4) мӢӨмҡ©м Ғ нғҖнҳ‘ вҖ” мҙҲкё°м—җлҠ” н•ҳмқҙлёҢлҰ¬л“ңлЎң мӢңмһ‘н•ҳм—¬ мӢңмһҘмқҙ мӨҖл№„лҗң нӣ„ м „кё°м°ЁлЎң м „нҷҳ
lessons.answer: Unreasonable вҖ” made the world adapt to their vision
lessons.explanation: Tesla was 'unreasonable' in Shaw's sense. The industry said full electric was impractical (charging infrastructure, range anxiety, battery cost). Tesla said: we'll build Superchargers, prove range works, and drive battery costs down. The world adapted вҖ” now every automaker is going electric. Unreasonable won.

title: Seven Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse
description: The a16z framework for open metaverse: decentralization, property rights, identity, composability, openness, community ownership, and social immersion

lessons.title: Decentralization as Foundation
lessons.content:
## The Fundamental Principle

Decentralization is the **dominant and fundamental principle** of a proper metaverse. A metaverse not owned or operated by a single entity or oligarchy.

## The Centralized Platform Trap

Centralized platforms follow a predictable lifecycle:

1. **Early phase:** Friendly and cooperative to attract users and developers
2. **Growth slows:** Relationship shifts from cooperative to competitive
3. **Extraction begins:** Zero-sum game вҖ” high fees, user rights abused, arbitrary bans

This is not evil вҖ” it's the rational behavior of a centralized entity optimizing for shareholders.

## Without Decentralization, You Can Be 'Rugged'

In a centralized metaverse:
- Your virtual land can be deleted
- Your items can be confiscated
- Your account can be banned
- The rules can change overnight
- You have no recourse

## Blockchain's 'Strong Commitments'

Blockchain enables **code-enforced commitments** that centralized platforms can never make:
- Smart contracts can't be unilaterally changed
- Assets are cryptographically yours
- Rules are transparent and verifiable
- Exit is always possible

Centralized promises are weak. Code commitments are strong.

## The Open vs Closed Choice

Two competing visions exist:
- **Open metaverse:** Decentralized, user-owned, interoperable, open
- **Closed metaverse:** Corporate-controlled, extractive, walled gardens

Decentralization is the dividing line.
key_ideas: Decentralization prevents single-entity control and capture
Centralized platforms start friendly, turn extractive as growth slows
Without decentralization, users can be 'rugged' at any time
Blockchain enables code-enforced commitments vs weak promises
Open vs closed metaverse is the fundamental choice
lessons.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)
lessons.exercise:
мӨ‘м•ҷнҷ” н”Ңлһ«нҸјмқҙ лё”лЎқмІҙмқё кё°л°ҳ л©”нғҖлІ„мҠӨмҷҖ лҸҷмқјн•ң м•ҪмҶҚмқ„ н•  мҲҳ м—ҶлҠ” мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) They can change rules unilaterally even if they promise not to
2) мӨ‘м•ҷнҷ” н”Ңлһ«нҸјмқҖ мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗ мҲҳк°Җ м Ғм–ҙ л„ӨнҠёмӣҢнҒ¬ нҡЁкіјлҘј лӢ¬м„ұн•ҳкё° м–ҙл ӨмӣҢм„ң
3) кё°мҲ м ҒмңјлЎң лё”лЎқмІҙмқё мҲҳмӨҖмқҳ нҠёлһңмһӯм…ҳ мІҳлҰ¬ м„ұлҠҘмқ„ кө¬нҳ„н•  мҲҳ м—Ҷм–ҙм„ң
4) мӨ‘м•ҷнҷ” н”Ңлһ«нҸјлҸ„ лҸҷмқјн•ң м•ҪмҶҚмқҙ к°ҖлҠҘн•ҳм§Җл§Ң к·ңм ң нҷҳкІҪмқҙ мқҙлҘј м ңн•ңн•ҙм„ң
lessons.answer: They can change rules unilaterally even if they promise not to
lessons.explanation: A centralized platform's promises are only as strong as its willingness to keep them. If the CEO changes, if investors demand profits, if regulators apply pressure вҖ” the rules can change. Blockchain commitments are enforced by code and cryptography, not corporate goodwill. That's the difference between weak and strong commitments.

lessons.title: Digital Property Rights
lessons.content:
## The Rental Economy

Today's successful games earn billions selling in-game items: skins, emotes, weapons, land. But players aren't buying вҖ” they're **renting**.

When you 'buy' a Fortnite skin:
- You can't sell it
- You can't trade it
- You can't use it in other games
- If the game shuts down, it's gone
- If you're banned, you lose access

You don't own it. You have a revocable license.

## The Physical World Logic

In the physical world, when you buy something, you own it:
- You can sell it
- You can trade it
- You can take it with you
- No one can arbitrarily take it away

Digital worlds should follow the same logic.

## The Technology: Cryptography + Blockchain + NFTs

Three innovations make digital property rights possible:

1. **Cryptography:** Proves ownership without central authority
2. **Blockchain:** Public, tamper-proof ledger of ownership
3. **NFTs:** Unique, transferable digital assets

## From Serfs to Homesteaders

The metaverse should convert **digital serfs into homesteaders**:
- Serfs: Work the land but don't own it, subject to lord's whims
- Homesteaders: Own their land, can sell it, can build on it

True property rights are the foundation of economic freedom.
key_ideas: Current in-game purchases are rentals, not ownership
True ownership means ability to sell, trade, and port
Physical world logic: buying = owning
Cryptography + blockchain + NFTs enable digital property rights
Goal: convert digital serfs into homesteaders
lessons.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)
lessons.exercise:
кІҢмһ„ лӮҙ м•„мқҙн…ң кө¬л§Өк°Җ мҶҢмң ліҙлӢӨ мһ„лҢҖм—җ лҚ” к°Җк№Ңмҡҙ мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) Because you can't sell, trade, or use it outside the game
2) кІҢмһ„ м•„мқҙн…ңмқҳ к°Җм№ҳк°Җ мӢңк°„мқҙ м§ҖлӮЁм—җ л”°лқј к°җмҶҢн•ҳлҠ” кө¬мЎ°м Ғ мқён”Ңл Ҳмқҙм…ҳ л•Ңл¬ё
3) кІҢмһ„мӮ¬к°Җ м•„мқҙн…ң кұ°лһҳм—җ лҶ’мқҖ мҲҳмҲҳлЈҢлҘј л¶Җкіјн•ҳм—¬ мӢӨм§Ҳм Ғ мҶҢмң к¶Ңмқҙ м ңн•ңлҗҳм–ҙм„ң
4) м•„мқҙн…ңмқҙ кІҢмһ„ м„ңлІ„м—җ м ҖмһҘлҗҳм§Җл§Ң мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗк°Җ м–ём ңл“  лӢӨлҘё кІҢмһ„мңјлЎң мқҙм „н•  мҲҳ мһҲм–ҙм„ң
lessons.answer: Because you can't sell, trade, or use it outside the game
lessons.explanation: Ownership means control. If the game company can revoke your access, prevent you from selling, or delete the item when the game shuts down, you don't own it вҖ” you're renting it. NFTs change this by making ownership cryptographically enforceable and transferable outside the original platform's control.

lessons.title: Self-Sovereign Identity
lessons.content:
## You Can't Own Property If You Don't Own Yourself

Property rights require identity. If someone else controls your identity, they control your property.

## The Surveillance Social Login Problem

Today's web uses centralized social login (Google, Meta, Apple):
- Convenient for users (one click)
- **Surveillance for platforms** (track you across the web)
- Data feeds advertising models
- Single point of control and failure

## Web3 Alternative: Self-Sovereign Identity

Your identity should:
- Persist across the metaverse without central providers
- Be verifiable without surveillance
- Be controlled by you, not corporations

## The Technology

**Wallets** (MetaMask, Phantom, Rainbow) provide cryptographic authentication:
- Private key proves identity
- No tracking required
- No central identity provider

**Standards** enable interoperability:
- **EIP-4361:** Sign-In with Ethereum (like OAuth but decentralized)
- **ENS:** Ethereum Name Service (human-readable names like vitalik.eth)

## The Philosophical Shift

Web2: 'Log in with Google to prove you're you'
Web3: 'Sign with your wallet to prove you control this key'

One requires trusting Google. The other requires only math.
key_ideas: Cannot own property without owning your identity
Current social login enables surveillance for ad targeting
Self-sovereign identity: persistent, verifiable, user-controlled
Wallets provide cryptographic authentication without tracking
Standards like EIP-4361 and ENS enable open identity
lessons.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)
lessons.exercise:
'Sign-In with Ethereum'мқҙ 'Sign-In with Google'кіј лӢӨлҘё м җмқҖ?

1) It doesn't require trusting a central identity provider to authenticate you
2) мқҙлҚ”лҰ¬мӣҖ лё”лЎқмІҙмқёмқҳ лҶ’мқҖ ліҙм•Ҳ мҲҳмӨҖмңјлЎң кі„м • н•ҙнӮ№ мң„н—ҳмқҙ кё°мҲ м ҒмңјлЎң л¶Ҳк°ҖлҠҘн•ҙм„ң
3) мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗмқҳ лЎңк·ёмқё мқҙл Ҙмқҙ мҳЁмІҙмқём—җ кё°лЎқлҗҳм–ҙ нҲ¬лӘ…н•ң к°җмӮ¬ м¶”м Ғмқҙ к°ҖлҠҘн•ҙм„ң
4) н•ҳлӮҳмқҳ м§Җк°‘ мЈјмҶҢлЎң лӘЁл“  мӣ№мӮ¬мқҙнҠём—җ м ‘мҶҚн•  мҲҳ мһҲм–ҙ мӮ¬мҡ© нҺёмқҳм„ұмқҙ лҚ” лҶ’м•„м„ң
lessons.answer: It doesn't require trusting a central identity provider to authenticate you
lessons.explanation: Sign-In with Google requires Google to verify your identity and can track you. Sign-In with Ethereum uses cryptographic signatures вҖ” you prove you control a private key, no middleman required. Google could deny you access or track your activity. Math can't.

lessons.title: Composability: Lego Blocks for Software
lessons.content:
## The Lego Analogy

Composability is the ability to combine software components like Lego blocks:
- Each component is written once
- Components can be freely combined
- New combinations create exponential value

## Weak vs Strong Composability

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|--------|
| **Weak** | Use APIs but can't modify internals | Stripe payments, Twilio messaging |
| **Strong** | Permissionless modification at all layers | DeFi protocols (Compound, Uniswap) |

Minecraft and Roblox let you create, but you can't fork the engine or change the economics. Stripe lets you accept payments, but you can't modify how fees work.

## DeFi: Strong Composability in Action

DeFi demonstrates strong composability:
- Anyone can build on Uniswap's live contracts
- You can fork Compound and modify its logic
- One protocol's output becomes another's input
- No permission required

**Example:** Yearn Finance built on top of Compound, Aave, Curve, and others вҖ” all permissionlessly composing live protocols.

## Exponential Value

Composability creates exponential growth like:
- **Compound interest** in finance
- **Moore's Law** in computing
- **Network effects** in platforms

Each new component multiplies value, not adds to it.
key_ideas: Composability: combining software like Lego blocks
Weak form: blackbox APIs you can use but not modify
Strong form: permissionless modification at all layers
DeFi exemplifies strong composability (Yearn on Compound/Aave/Curve)
Creates exponential value like compound interest or Moore's Law
lessons.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)
lessons.exercise:
DeFiмқҳ мЎ°н•©м„ұ(Composability)мқҙ StripeліҙлӢӨ 'Strong'н•ң мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) DeFi allows permissionless forking and modification, Stripe doesn't
2) DeFi н”„лЎңнҶ мҪңмқҙ лҚ” л§ҺмқҖ API м—”л“ңнҸ¬мқёнҠёлҘј м ңкіөн•ҳм—¬ нҶөн•© мҳөм…ҳмқҙ лӢӨм–‘н•ҙм„ң
3) DeFiмқҳ мҠӨл§ҲнҠё м»ЁнҠёлһҷнҠёк°Җ Stripeмқҳ кІ°м ң мІҳлҰ¬ мҶҚлҸ„ліҙлӢӨ л№ лҘҙкІҢ мӢӨн–үлҗҳм–ҙм„ң
4) DeFiлҠ” мӨ‘м•ҷнҷ” м„ңл№„мҠӨмҷҖ лӢ¬лҰ¬ м„ңл№„мҠӨ мӨ‘лӢЁ м—Ҷмқҙ 24мӢңк°„ мҡҙмҳҒмқҙ к°ҖлҠҘн•ҙм„ң
lessons.answer: DeFi allows permissionless forking and modification, Stripe doesn't
lessons.explanation: Stripe provides an API вҖ” you can use their service, but you can't change how it works or fork it to create your own version. DeFi protocols are open source and permissionless вҖ” you can fork Uniswap, modify its fee structure, add features, and deploy your own version. That's the difference between weak (use-only) and strong (modify-and-remix) composability.

lessons.title: Openness and Open Source
lessons.content:
## Why Open Source Matters

True composability is impossible without open source. If code is closed:
- You can't inspect it
- You can't modify it
- You can't remix it
- You can't verify it

## The Benefits of Transparency

When code, algorithms, and protocols are transparent public goods:

**1. More sophisticated experiences:** Builders can deeply integrate rather than surface-level API calls

**2. More trustworthy systems:** Open code can be audited by anyone

**3. Clear economic conditions:** All parties see the same rules

**4. Reduced information asymmetry:** No hidden fees, no surprise changes

## Web3 Composability = Open Source Culture

The power of Web3 composability stems from open source:
- DeFi: All major protocols are open source
- NFTs: Token standards are public
- DAOs: Governance code is transparent

This isn't just philosophy вҖ” it's practical. Closed systems can't achieve the same level of permissionless innovation.

## The Virtuous Cycle

Openness вҶ’ Composability вҶ’ Innovation вҶ’ More openness

Each open protocol becomes infrastructure for the next wave of builders.
key_ideas: Open source is prerequisite for true composability
Transparency enables sophisticated, trustworthy experiences
Economic conditions become clear to all parties
Reduces information asymmetry and hidden surprises
Web3 composability depends on open source culture
lessons.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)
lessons.exercise:
л№„кіөк°ң мҶҢмҠӨ мҶҢн”„нҠёмӣЁм–ҙк°Җ мҳӨн”ҲмҶҢмҠӨмҷҖ лҸҷмқјн•ң мҲҳмӨҖмқҳ мЎ°н•©м„ұмқ„ лӢ¬м„ұн•  мҲҳ м—ҶлҠ” мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) You can't inspect, modify, or verify closed code
2) л№„кіөк°ң мҶҢмҠӨлҠ” лқјмқҙм„ мҠӨ л№„мҡ©мқҙ лҶ’м•„ мҶҢк·ңлӘЁ к°ңл°ңмһҗк°Җ нҶөн•©н•ҳкё° м–ҙл ӨмӣҢм„ң
3) л№„кіөк°ң мҶҢмҠӨмқҳ м—…лҚ°мқҙнҠё мЈјкё°к°Җ лҠҗл Ө л№ лҘҙкІҢ ліҖн•ҳлҠ” мғқнғңкі„м—җ м Ғмқ‘н•ҳкё° нһҳл“Өм–ҙм„ң
4) л№„кіөк°ң мҶҢмҠӨ кё°м—…мқҙ кІҪмҹҒмӮ¬мҷҖмқҳ нҶөн•©мқ„ м „лһөм ҒмңјлЎң кұ°л¶Җн•ҳлҠ” кІҪн–Ҙмқҙ мһҲм–ҙм„ң
lessons.answer: You can't inspect, modify, or verify closed code
lessons.explanation: Composability requires being able to understand how components work, modify them for your needs, and verify they're safe. Closed source is a black box вҖ” you can only use it as-is, hoping it does what it claims. Open source lets you read the code, fork it, audit it, and build on it with full knowledge. That's why DeFi composability is possible.

lessons.title: Community Ownership and Governance
lessons.content:
## Beyond Corporate Edicts

In a true metaverse, stakeholders should have governance voice **proportional to their participation** вҖ” not subject to product manager edicts.

## The Disneyland Analogy

If a single entity owns a virtual world:
- It might be delightful (like Disneyland)
- But it's fundamentally limited
- The owner's interests take priority
- Users are guests, not citizens

## Tokens as Coordination Mechanism

**Tokens** align network participants toward common goals:
- Builders get tokens for contributions
- Creators earn tokens from work
- Investors stake tokens on success
- Users gain tokens through participation

Everyone's incentives point in the same direction.

## DAOs: Governance Experiments

DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations) enable:
- Flexible governance without corporate rigidity
- Democratic participation in decisions
- Informal but effective coordination
- Experimentation with new models

## The Miracle of Alignment

Community ownership creates a **miracle of coordination**:
- No CEO to subpoena
- No board to lobby
- No single point of control
- But effective collective action

This was impossible before blockchain and tokens.
key_ideas: Stakeholders govern proportional to participation, not corporate edict
Single-entity ownership creates asymmetric power (Disneyland model)
Tokens align builders, creators, investors, users toward common good
DAOs enable flexible, democratic governance experiments
Community ownership prevents capture while enabling coordination
lessons.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)
lessons.exercise:
нҶ нҒ°мқҙ лӢӨм–‘н•ң мқҙн•ҙкҙҖкі„мһҗ к°„мқҳ мқҙн•ҙлҘј м–ҙл–»кІҢ м •л ¬мӢңнӮӨлҠ”к°Җ?

1) Everyone's token value increases if the network succeeds, aligning interests
2) нҶ нҒ° ліҙмң лҹүм—җ л№„лЎҖн•ҳм—¬ кұ°лІ„л„ҢмҠӨ нҲ¬н‘ңк¶Ңмқҙ л¶Җм—¬лҗҳм–ҙ мқҳмӮ¬кІ°м • м°ём—¬к°Җ ліҙмһҘлҗҳлҜҖлЎң
3) нҶ нҒ°мқҙ н”Ңлһ«нҸј м„ңл№„мҠӨ мқҙмҡ©мқҳ н•„мҲҳ кІ°м ң мҲҳлӢЁмңјлЎң кё°лҠҘн•ҳм—¬ мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗлҘј лқҪмқён•ҳлҜҖлЎң
4) мҠӨн…ҢмқҙнӮ№ л©”м»ӨлӢҲмҰҳмқ„ нҶөн•ҙ м•…мқҳм Ғ н–үмң„мһҗм—җкІҢ кІҪм ңм Ғ мІҳлІҢмқ„ л¶Җкіјн•  мҲҳ мһҲмңјлҜҖлЎң
lessons.answer: Everyone's token value increases if the network succeeds, aligning interests
lessons.explanation: Tokens create what's called 'incentive alignment.' If you're a builder, creator, investor, or user holding tokens, you all want the same thing: the network to succeed and the token to gain value. This aligns interests that would normally conflict вҖ” users want low fees, platforms want high revenue. With tokens, everyone wins together.

lessons.title: Social Immersion (Not VR Hardware)
lessons.content:
## The Trojan Horse Narrative

Big Tech companies claim high-performance VR/AR hardware is **essential** for the metaverse. This is a Trojan horse.

Their real goal: become the dominant computing interface provider for 3D virtual worlds вҖ” like Microsoft dominated PC operating systems.

## The Metaverse Doesn't Require VR/AR

What actually matters is **broad social immersion**:
- Hanging out remotely
- Working together
- Socializing with friends
- Having fun
- **Earning a living**

You can do all of this on Discord, Twitter Spaces, Clubhouse, Zoom вҖ” no headset required.

## The Pandemic Lesson

COVID-19 proved the demand for immersive social experiences beyond email:
- Zoom usage exploded
- Discord became default for communities
- Remote work became permanent

None of this required VR.

## What Metaverse Really Needs

Not hardware, but:
- Property rights (earn and own)
- Self-sovereign identity (persistent you)
- Community ownership (governance voice)

These enable you to build a life, business, and status вҖ” the real 'immersion.'

## View-Agnostic

The metaverse is **view-agnostic**: access via phone, browser, VR, AR, or hologram. What matters is the persistent world and social fabric, not the viewport.
key_ideas: VR/AR hardware not essential for metaverse
Big Tech's hardware push is bid to control computing interface
Social immersion possible via Discord, Zoom, Twitter Spaces
Pandemic proved demand for immersive social beyond email
Real immersion comes from property rights, identity, and governance
lessons.paper_ref: a16z crypto, '7 Essential Ingredients of a Metaverse' (2022)
lessons.exercise:
a16zк°Җ VR н•ҳл“ңмӣЁм–ҙ мҡ”кө¬мӮ¬н•ӯмқ„ 'нҠёлЎңмқҙ лӘ©л§Ҳ'лқјкі  л¶ҖлҘҙлҠ” мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) It's a strategy to control the metaverse computing platform
2) VR н•ҳл“ңмӣЁм–ҙмқҳ лҶ’мқҖ к°ҖкІ©мқҙ л©”нғҖлІ„мҠӨ м ‘к·јм„ұмқ„ мқҳлҸ„м ҒмңјлЎң м ңн•ңн•ҳм—¬ н”„лҰ¬лҜём—„ мӢңмһҘмқ„ нҳ•м„ұн•ҳлҜҖлЎң
3) л№…н…ҢнҒ¬ кё°м—…мқҙ VR кё°мҲ  нҠ№н—ҲлҘј лҸ…м җн•ҳм—¬ кІҪмҹҒмһҗмқҳ мӢңмһҘ м§„мһ…мқ„ м°ЁлӢЁн•ҳл ӨлҠ” м „лһөмқҙлҜҖлЎң
4) VR н•ҳл“ңмӣЁм–ҙк°Җ мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗ н–үлҸҷ лҚ°мқҙн„°лҘј мҲҳм§‘н•ҳлҠ” к°җмӢң лҸ„кө¬лЎң нҷңмҡ©лҗ  мҲҳ мһҲмңјлҜҖлЎң
lessons.answer: It's a strategy to control the metaverse computing platform
lessons.explanation: If Meta or Apple can convince everyone that the metaverse requires their VR headset, they become the gatekeepers вҖ” like Microsoft with Windows or Apple with iOS. They control the platform, extract fees, and set rules. The 'social immersion' argument says: you don't need their hardware. Discord and Zoom already provide immersion. The metaverse is software and social fabric, not headsets.

title: Go-to-Market in Web3
description: How tokens solve cold start problems, stakeholder diversity, and DAO-specific GTM strategies in DeFi, social, and gaming

lessons.title: The Cold Start Problem
lessons.content:
## Every Business Faces This

How do you start from nothing?
- How do you get your first customers?
- How do you create network effects?
- How do you compete when incumbents have millions of users?

This is the **cold start problem**.

## Web2 Solution: Sales and Marketing

Traditional companies invest heavily in:
- Sales teams (enterprise) or growth teams (consumer)
- Marketing campaigns and lead generation
- Customer acquisition cost (CAC) optimization
- Retention and lifetime value (LTV) analysis

It works, but it's expensive and slow.

## The Network Effects Paradox

Networks are most valuable when they're large, but they're hardest to grow when they're small:
- Empty marketplace: no buyers attract sellers, no sellers attract buyers
- Social network: nobody joins because nobody's there
- Platform: developers won't build without users, users won't come without apps

## The Traditional Approach

Companies solve this by:
1. **Subsidizing one side** (Uber pays drivers, absorbs losses)
2. **Acquiring users** (spend millions on ads)
3. **Building network slowly** (years of patient growth)

## Enter Web3 and Tokens

Tokens provide an alternative: **reward early users before network effects kick in**.

Instead of spending $50M on marketing, distribute $50M in tokens to early participants. They become owners and evangelists, not just customers.
key_ideas: Cold start problem: how to bootstrap network from nothing
Web2 solution: invest heavily in sales/marketing teams
Network effects paradox: most valuable when large, hardest to grow when small
Traditional approach: subsidize, acquire, or grow slowly
Web3 alternative: tokens reward early participation and create ownership
lessons.exercise:
мҪңл“ң мҠӨнғҖнҠё л¬ём ңк°Җ л„ӨнҠёмӣҢнҒ¬ нҡЁкіј л№„мҰҲлӢҲмҠӨм—җм„ң к°ҖмһҘ м–ҙл Өмҡҙ мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) They're most valuable when large but have no value when small
2) мҙҲкё° мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗлҘј нҷ•ліҙн•ҳкё° мң„н•ң л§ҲмјҖнҢ… л№„мҡ©мқҙ м „нҶөм Ғ л№„мҰҲлӢҲмҠӨліҙлӢӨ кё°н•ҳкёүмҲҳм ҒмңјлЎң лҶ’м•„м„ң
3) л„ӨнҠёмӣҢнҒ¬ нҡЁкіј л№„мҰҲлӢҲмҠӨлҠ” к·ңм ң мҠ№мқёмқ„ л°ӣкё° м–ҙл ӨмӣҢ мҙҲкё° м„ұмһҘмқҙ лІ•м ҒмңјлЎң м ңн•ңлҗҳм–ҙм„ң
4) кІҪмҹҒ н”Ңлһ«нҸјмқҙ мқҙлҜё к°•л Ҙн•ң л„ӨнҠёмӣҢнҒ¬ нҡЁкіјлҘј ліҙмң н•ҳкі  мһҲм–ҙ мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗ м „нҷҳ л№„мҡ©мқҙ лҶ’м•„м„ң
lessons.answer: They're most valuable when large but have no value when small
lessons.explanation: A marketplace with 1 buyer and 1 seller is nearly useless. A social network with 10 users is pointless. But with millions, they're incredibly valuable. This creates a paradox: you need users to attract users. Web2 solves this with massive subsidies and marketing spend. Web3 solves it by making early users into owners.

lessons.title: Tokens as GTM Catalyst
lessons.content:
## The Compound Example

In June 2020, Compound launched **liquidity mining**: users who lent or borrowed received COMP governance tokens as rewards.

Result: TVL (Total Value Locked) skyrocketed from ~$100M to ~$600M in weeks.

## How Tokens Solve Cold Start

Tokens reward early users for contributing **before** network effects exist:
- First lenders on Compound got extra COMP rewards
- First users on a new DEX get higher token allocations
- Early community members get tokens for participation

They're compensated for taking risk on an unproven network.

## From Customers to Evangelists

Token holders don't just use the protocol вҖ” they:
- Promote it (their tokens gain value if it succeeds)
- Recruit others (grow the network)
- Contribute ideas (governance rights)
- Defend it (invested in success)

Early users become more powerful than Web2 salespeople.

## The Caveat: Not Sufficient for Retention

Tokens **attract** users, but don't automatically make them sticky:
- Mercenary capital chases highest yields
- When token rewards dry up, users may leave
- Product-market fit still required

**Lesson:** Tokens bootstrap networks, but retention requires actual value.

## Web2 vs Web3 GTM

| Aspect | Web2 | Web3 |
|--------|------|------|
| **Spend $50M on** | Marketing/sales | Token distribution |
| **Early users are** | Customers | Owners + evangelists |
| **Retention from** | Product value | Product + ownership |
| **Network growth** | Paid acquisition | Aligned incentives |
key_ideas: Compound liquidity mining drove TVL from $100M to $600M
Tokens reward pre-network-effect contributions
Early users become evangelists more effective than salespeople
Caveat: tokens attract but don't guarantee retention
Product-market fit still required beyond token incentives
lessons.exercise:
нҶ нҒ° ліҙмң  мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗк°Җ м „нҶөм Ғ кі к°қліҙлӢӨ л„ӨнҠёмӣҢнҒ¬ м„ұмһҘм—җ лҚ” нҡЁкіјм Ғмқё мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) Their tokens gain value if the network succeeds, so they actively recruit and promote
2) нҶ нҒ° ліҙмң мһҗлҠ” кұ°лІ„л„ҢмҠӨ нҲ¬н‘ңк¶Ңмқ„ н–үмӮ¬н•ҳм—¬ м„ұмһҘ м „лһөмқ„ м§Ғм ‘ кІ°м •н•  мҲҳ мһҲмңјлҜҖлЎң
3) нҶ нҒ° мҠӨн…ҢмқҙнӮ№мңјлЎң мқён•ҙ н”Ңлһ«нҸјмқ„ л– лӮҳкё° м–ҙл ӨмӣҢ мһҘкё°м Ғ мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗ мң м§ҖмңЁмқҙ лҶ’м•„м„ң
4) нҶ нҒ° м—җм–ҙл“ңлЎӯ н”„лЎңк·ёлһЁмқҙ л°”мқҙлҹҙ л§ҲмјҖнҢ… нҡЁкіјлҘј мһҗлҸҷмңјлЎң мғқм„ұн•ҳлҠ” кө¬мЎ°мқҙлҜҖлЎң
lessons.answer: Their tokens gain value if the network succeeds, so they actively recruit and promote
lessons.explanation: Token holders have skin in the game. If they recruit 100 new users and the network grows, their tokens become more valuable. Traditional customers have no incentive to promote a product beyond personal goodwill. Token holders are financially aligned to become evangelists, recruiters, and defenders. That's why they're more powerful than salespeople.

lessons.title: Web3 GTM Stakeholders
lessons.content:
## Web2 vs Web3 Stakeholders

| Web2 | Web3 |
|------|------|
| Customers | Customers |
| | Developers |
| | Investors |
| | Partners |

Web2 GTM focuses narrowly on customer acquisition through sales and marketing.

Web3 GTM must coordinate **four stakeholder groups** simultaneously.

## Community > Sales/Marketing

Web3 organizations prioritize **community roles** over traditional sales:
- Community managers
- Developer advocates
- Governance coordinators
- Discord/forum moderators

Why? Because stakeholders are also owners, not just users.

## Purpose > Product

Web2 asks: 'What's the product?'
Web3 asks: 'What's the purpose?'

A clear purpose can attract community **before** the product exists:
- ConstitutionDAO: $47M raised to buy the Constitution (no product)
- Krause House: Community formed around goal of buying NBA team
- Purpose precedes execution

## Come for Purpose, Stay for Community

Web2: 'Come for the tool, stay for the network'
Web3: 'Come for the purpose, stay for community ownership'

The product doesn't have to be first. Purpose + community + governance can bootstrap everything else.

## The Stakeholder Balancing Act

**Developers** need tooling and docs
**Investors** need tokenomics clarity
**Partners** need integration support
**Customers** need product value

All four must be served вҖ” not just customers.
key_ideas: Web2 GTM targets customers; Web3 targets customers + developers + investors + partners
Community roles more important than sales/marketing roles
Purpose can precede product (ConstitutionDAO, Krause House)
Come for purpose, stay for community ownership
Must balance needs of four stakeholder groups simultaneously
lessons.exercise:
Web3 GTMм—җм„ң 'м„ёмқјмҰҲ'ліҙлӢӨ 'м»Өл®ӨлӢҲнӢ°'к°Җ лҚ” мӨ‘мҡ”н•ң мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) Stakeholders are owners, not just users, so community engagement is core to governance
2) Web3 н”„лЎңм қнҠёлҠ” мҳӨн”ҲмҶҢмҠӨмқҙлҜҖлЎң лі„лҸ„мқҳ м„ёмқјмҰҲ нҢҖ м—ҶмқҙлҸ„ к°ңл°ңмһҗк°Җ мһҗл°ңм ҒмңјлЎң мұ„нғқн•ҳм—¬м„ң
3) нғҲмӨ‘м•ҷнҷ” н”„лЎңнҶ мҪңмқҖ B2B м„ёмқјмҰҲ кө¬мЎ°м—җ м Ғн•©н•ҳм§Җ м•Ҡм•„ м»Өл®ӨлӢҲнӢ° кё°л°ҳ м„ұмһҘмқҙ мң мқјн•ң л°©лІ•мқҙлҜҖлЎң
4) нҶ нҒ° ліҙмң мһҗк°Җ н”„лЎңнҶ мҪң мҲҳмҲҳлЈҢ мҲҳмқөмқ„ м§Ғм ‘ л¶„л°°л°ӣмңјлҜҖлЎң м„ёмқјмҰҲ лҢҖмӢ  мҲҳмқө кіөмң к°Җ н•өмӢ¬мқҙм–ҙм„ң
lessons.answer: Stakeholders are owners, not just users, so community engagement is core to governance
lessons.explanation: In Web2, customers buy a product and leave. In Web3, token holders are owners who govern, contribute, and promote. Community engagement isn't marketing вҖ” it's governance, coordination, and ownership activation. You're not selling to customers; you're coordinating co-owners. That's why community roles matter more than traditional sales.

lessons.title: DeFi DAO GTM Strategies
lessons.content:
## The Progressive Decentralization Path

Most DeFi projects follow this sequence:

1. **Centralized dev team** builds and launches protocol
2. **Governance token** launched
3. **DAO** formed
4. **Control transferred** to DAO

This path balances speed (centralized launch) with decentralization (DAO ownership).

## Case Study: MakerDAO

- **2015:** Launched as DAO
- **2018:** Foundation created for legal/operational structure
- **2021:** Foundation retired, full DAO control

**Purpose:** Dai stablecoin as fast, low-cost, borderless stable value unit

**Governance:** MKR token holders approve protocol changes, collateral ratios, risk parameters

## Case Study: Uniswap

- Launched by centralized company (Uniswap Labs)
- UNI governance token distributed to users
- Now controlled by Uniswap DAO
- Uniswap Labs is just **one contributor** among many

## The TVL Trap

**TVL (Total Value Locked)** is the canonical DeFi success metric, but it's misleading:
- High yield + open source code = easy to attract TVL
- 'Vampire attacks' copy code and offer higher rewards
- TVL can disappear as fast as it arrived

## Better Metrics

- **Unique token holders** (ownership distribution)
- **Community engagement** (forum posts, votes, Discord activity)
- **Developer activity** (GitHub commits, forks)
- **Integrations** (how many other protocols build on yours)

Integrations especially matter вҖ” being infrastructure means stickiness.

## The Value Accrual Difference

Web2 platform: Value accrues to shareholders
DeFi DAO: Value accrues to token holders (who are also users/builders)
key_ideas: Path: centralized dev вҶ’ governance token вҶ’ DAO вҶ’ control transfer
MakerDAO (DAO вҶ’ Foundation вҶ’ DAO), Uniswap (company вҶ’ DAO)
TVL is canonical but misleading metric (easily gamed)
Better: token holders, community engagement, developer activity, integrations
Value accrues to token holders who are users/builders, not external shareholders
lessons.exercise:
TVL(Total Value Locked)мқҙ DeFi н”„лЎңнҶ мҪң кұҙм „м„ұмқҳ мҳӨн•ҙлҘј л¶Ҳлҹ¬мқјмңјнӮӨлҠ” м§Җн‘ңмқё мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) High rewards can attract mercenary capital that leaves when yields drop
2) TVLмқҖ н”„лЎңнҶ мҪң к°„ мӨ‘ліө мҳҲм№ҳлҘј кі л Өн•ҳм§Җ м•Ҡм•„ мӢӨм ң мһҗкёҲ к·ңлӘЁлҘј кіјлҢҖнҸүк°Җн•ҳлҜҖлЎң
3) мҠӨл§ҲнҠё м»ЁнҠёлһҷнҠёмқҳ ліҙм•Ҳ к°җмӮ¬ мҲҳмӨҖмқ„ л°ҳмҳҒн•ҳм§Җ м•Ҡм•„ мһ мһ¬м Ғ н•ҙнӮ№ мң„н—ҳмқ„ мҲЁкё°лҜҖлЎң
4) мҠӨн…Ңмқҙлё”мҪ”мқёкіј ліҖлҸҷм„ұ мһҗмӮ°мқҳ к°Җм№ҳлҘј лҸҷмқјн•ҳкІҢ м·Ёкёүн•ҳм—¬ лҰ¬мҠӨнҒ¬лҘј мҷңкіЎн•ҳлҜҖлЎң
lessons.answer: High rewards can attract mercenary capital that leaves when yields drop
lessons.explanation: TVL can be 'rented' with high token rewards. When SushiSwap launched, it copied Uniswap's code and offered higher SUSHI rewards. TVL flooded in, then partially left when rewards normalized. Sustainable metrics measure sticky users (token holders, active governance, integrations) not temporary capital chasing yields.

lessons.title: Social and Cultural DAO GTM
lessons.content:
## Purpose First, Method Later

Social DAOs flip the Web2 crowdfunding model:

**Web2 (Kickstarter):**
- Detailed plan: what you'll build, how, when
- Budget breakdown
- Execution timeline
- Risk analysis

**Web3 (Social DAO):**
- **Purpose:** Crystal clear
- **Method:** Figure it out together
- Community forms around shared belief

## Case Study: ConstitutionDAO

**Purpose:** Buy an original copy of the U.S. Constitution

**How it started:**
- No roadmap
- No execution plan
- No token (added after bid failed)
- Just a clear, compelling purpose

**Result:** $47M raised in weeks from thousands of strangers

**Why it worked:** Purpose was so clearly aligned that people contributed and promoted without needing details.

## Case Study: Krause House

**Purpose:** Buy an NBA team, pioneer fan governance

**Reality:** NBA teams cost $1B+. Krause House raised ~$4M.

**Pivot:** Bought stake in BIG3 basketball team instead (more achievable)

**Lesson:** Purpose attracts, but execution must adapt to reality.

## Case Study: Friends With Benefits (FWB)

**Started:** Token-gated Discord (must hold $FWB to join)

**Entry:** Minimum token amount + written application

**Evolution:** Community growth led to product (token-gating event app)

**Model:** Community вҶ’ Product (reverse of Web2)

## Key Metrics for Social DAOs

- Discord channel activity
- Member activation and retention
- Governance participation (votes, proposals)
- Paid contributors (how many actually work)
- Real-world events and IRL meetups
- Relationships formed (trust, collaboration)

Harder to quantify than DeFi, but these signal genuine community.
key_ideas: Purpose first, method later (vs Web2's detailed plans)
ConstitutionDAO: $47M with no roadmap, just clear purpose
Krause House: Purpose attracts, but execution must adapt
FWB: Community вҶ’ Product (reverse of Web2)
Metrics: Discord activity, governance participation, paid contributors
lessons.exercise:
ConstitutionDAOк°Җ нӮҘмҠӨнғҖн„° мә нҺҳмқёкіј лӢӨлҘё м җмқҖ?

1) ConstitutionDAO had a clearer purpose with no detailed execution plan; Kickstarter requires both
2) ConstitutionDAOлҠ” нҶ нҒ° л°ңн–үмқ„ нҶөн•ҙ м°ём—¬мһҗм—җкІҢ кұ°лІ„л„ҢмҠӨ нҲ¬н‘ңк¶Ңмқ„ л¶Җм—¬н•ң л°ҳл©ҙ нӮҘмҠӨнғҖн„°лҠ” мқјл°©м Ғ нӣ„мӣҗ кө¬мЎ°
3) ConstitutionDAOлҠ” мҠӨл§ҲнҠём»ЁнҠёлһҷнҠёлЎң мһҗкёҲмқҙ мһҗлҸҷ кҙҖлҰ¬лҗҳм–ҙ мӨ‘к°„ мҲҳмҲҳлЈҢк°Җ м—Ҷм—Ҳм§Җл§Ң нӮҘмҠӨнғҖн„°лҠ” н”Ңлһ«нҸј мҲҳмҲҳлЈҢ л¶Җкіј
4) ConstitutionDAOлҠ” м•”нҳёнҷ”нҸҗлЎңл§Ң кё°л¶ҖлҘј л°ӣм•„ кёҖлЎңлІҢ м°ём—¬к°Җ к°ҖлҠҘн–Ҳм§Җл§Ң нӮҘмҠӨнғҖн„°лҠ” лІ•м •нҷ”нҸҗл§Ң м§Җмӣҗ
lessons.answer: ConstitutionDAO had a clearer purpose with no detailed execution plan; Kickstarter requires both
lessons.explanation: Kickstarter forces you to specify exactly what you'll build, how much each component costs, delivery timeline, etc. ConstitutionDAO said: 'We're going to try to buy the Constitution.' No detailed plan. No token initially. Just purpose. People aligned so strongly they contributed $47M in weeks. That's the power of purpose-first coordination.

title: Scissor Labels and Narrative Wars
description: Understanding how ambiguous trend labels become battlegrounds for narrative control in Web3 and beyond

lessons.title: What Are Scissor Labels?
lessons.content:
## The Hyperpop Example

In 2020, 'Hyperpop' emerged as a music trend label associated with artists like A.G. Cook and SOPHIE. It gained massive recognition.

But **no one could agree what Hyperpop actually was.**

Laura Les (100 gecs): 'Everyone draws the line at a different spot.'
Vice: 'A genre tag for genre-less music.'

Yet thousands of hours were spent debating: 'Is [artist X] Hyperpop or not?'

## The Definition

**Scissor Label:** A term that:
1. Becomes widely recognized as a trend name
2. Lacks an agreed-upon definition
3. Appears to have a specific definition but doesn't
4. Emerges accidentally, not by design

Loosely based on Scott Alexander's 'scissor statements' (truth claims designed to create maximum disagreement).

## The Power Dynamics

**Controlling the definition = controlling the trend narrative.**

If your work is 'inside' the Hyperpop label, you're part of the trend. If you're 'outside,' you're not.

This matters when:
- VCs fund startups based on narrative fit ('Is this Spatial Software?')
- Cultural capital accrues to trend insiders
- Communities define boundaries and membership

## Other Examples

- **Metaverse:** Everyone claims to build it; no consensus on definition
- **Web3:** Is it blockchain? Crypto? Decentralization? All of the above?
- **Spatial Software:** John Palmer's term that everyone wanted to claim
- **Normcore, Hipster, Vaporwave:** Past scissor labels now cheap-feeling

## Why They Emerge

Scissor labels aren't created вҖ” they're **discovered** when:
- Something genuinely interesting emerges organically
- Everyone recognizes there's a 'there' there
- But no one can pin down exactly what 'it' is
- The vacuum of definition becomes a power struggle
key_ideas: Scissor label: widely recognized trend name, no agreed definition
Emerges accidentally when something interesting appears organically
Controlling definition = controlling trend narrative
Examples: Hyperpop, Metaverse, Web3, Spatial Software
Not designed but discovered; definition vacuum creates power struggle
lessons.exercise:
'л©”нғҖлІ„мҠӨ'к°Җ Scissor Labelмқё мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) Everyone uses the term but definitions vary wildly (VR worlds? NFT games? Virtual real estate?)
2) л©”нғҖ(Meta) мӮ¬к°Җ мғҒн‘ңк¶Ңмқ„ мЈјмһҘн•ҳм—¬ лӢӨлҘё кё°м—…мқҙ мқҙ мҡ©м–ҙлҘј мӮ¬мҡ©н•ҳлҠ” лҚ° лІ•м Ғ л¶„мҹҒмқҙ л°ңмғқн•ҙм„ң
3) кё°мҲ м ҒмңјлЎң м•„м§Ғ кө¬нҳ„мқҙ л¶Ҳк°ҖлҠҘн•ҳм—¬ мӢӨмІҙк°Җ м—ҶлҠ” л§ҲмјҖнҢ… лІ„мҰҲмӣҢл“ңм—җ л¶Ҳкіјн•ҙм„ң
4) кІҢмһ„ мӮ°м—…кіј лё”лЎқмІҙмқё мӮ°м—…мқҙ м„ңлЎң лӢӨлҘё кё°мҲ  мҠӨнғқмқ„ кё°л°ҳмңјлЎң кІҪмҹҒн•ҳкі  мһҲм–ҙм„ң
lessons.answer: Everyone uses the term but definitions vary wildly (VR worlds? NFT games? Virtual real estate?)
lessons.explanation: 'Metaverse' became the defining tech trend of 2021-2022. But ask 10 people what it means: Meta says VR headsets, a16z says open digital worlds, gaming companies say persistent online games, NFT projects say virtual real estate. No consensus. Controlling the definition matters вҖ” it determines what projects get funded, what's 'legitimate,' what's hype. Classic scissor label.

lessons.title: Narrative Wars and Real Stakes
lessons.content:
## The Disguise

Scissor label debates **disguise power struggles as intellectual conversations.**

The surface debate: 'What is Hyperpop?'
The real stakes: Preserving a queer/trans-friendly creative community space.

The surface debate: 'What is Spatial Software?'
The real stakes: Startup funding (does my company fit the VC narrative?).

## The VC Narrative Dynamic

In venture-backed tech:
- VCs invest in **narratives** (themes like 'AI', 'Web3', 'Metaverse')
- Startups must fit into funded narratives to raise capital
- Being 'inside' a hot scissor label = access to funding
- Being 'outside' = ignored

**Example:** 'Spatial Software' (John Palmer's term) became a VC narrative. Founders scrambled to position their apps as 'spatial' to access that capital.

## The Community Preservation Dynamic

Hyperpop wasn't just about genre classification:
- The scene was unusually welcoming to queer/trans artists
- As the label went mainstream, definition battles were about **protecting that space**
- 'Gatekeeping' was actually community preservation

## The Binary Trap

Scissor label debates follow a binary pattern:
'Is X a Metaverse project or not?'
'Is Y a DeFi protocol or not?'

This binary framing:
- Hides the real stakes (funding, community, cultural capital)
- Makes productive discussion nearly impossible
- Wastes time on unanswerable questions

## Recognizing Narrative Wars

Signal: Debate is **strictly binary** (in or out, yes or no)

If you're arguing whether something 'counts,' you're probably in a scissor label narrative war.

The real question: **What's actually at stake beyond the definition?**
key_ideas: Scissor labels disguise power struggles as intellectual debates
Real stakes: funding, community preservation, cultural capital
VC narrative inclusion affects startup fundraising
Hyperpop debate was about protecting queer/trans community space
Binary framing (is X included?) hides nuanced real stakes
lessons.exercise:
Hyperpop м •мқҳ л…јмҹҒмқҙ лӢЁмҲңн•ң мқҢм•… л¶„лҘҳлҘј л„ҳм–ҙ мӨ‘мҡ”н–ҲлҚҳ мқҙмң лҠ”?

1) The scene was a welcoming space for queer/trans artists, and definition control meant community preservation
2) л Ҳмқҙлё” м •мқҳм—җ л”°лқј мҠӨнҠёлҰ¬л°Қ н”Ңлһ«нҸјмқҳ м•Ңкі лҰ¬мҰҳ м¶”мІңмқҙ лӢ¬лқјм ё м•„нӢ°мҠӨнҠё мҲҳмқөм—җ м§Ғм ‘м Ғ мҳҒн–Ҙмқ„ лҜёміӨмңјлҜҖлЎң
3) мқҢм•… л№„нҸүк°ҖмҷҖ нҢ¬ м»Өл®ӨлӢҲнӢ° к°„мқҳ л¬ёнҷ”м Ғ к¶Ңмң„лҘј л‘ҳлҹ¬мӢј м „нҶөм Ғ к°Ҳл“ұмқҙ л””м§Җн„ё кіөк°„мңјлЎң мқҙм „лҗң мӮ¬лЎҖмқҙлҜҖлЎң
4) мһҘлҘҙ м •мқҳк°Җ мқҢл°ҳмӮ¬мқҳ л§ҲмјҖнҢ… мҳҲмӮ° л°°л¶„кіј нҺҳмҠӨнӢ°лІҢ лқјмқём—… кІ°м •м—җ мӢӨм§Ҳм ҒмңјлЎң мҳҒн–Ҙмқ„ лҜёміӨмңјлҜҖлЎң
lessons.answer: The scene was a welcoming space for queer/trans artists, and definition control meant community preservation
lessons.explanation: The Hyperpop debate looked like arguing about genre boundaries. But the real stakes: the scene had become an unusually safe and welcoming space for queer and trans artists. As it went mainstream, 'gatekeeping' the definition was actually about preserving that community character. This is why scissor label debates are never just academic вҖ” real community and cultural stakes hide underneath.

lessons.title: Future Orientation vs Scissor Debate
lessons.content:
## Two Productive Moves

When you encounter a scissor label debate, you can:

### Move 1: Surface the Real Stakes

Ignore the binary question ('Is X included?'). Ask:
- What's actually being fought over?
- Who benefits from which definition?
- What's at stake beyond categorization?

Bring the real debate to the surface. This shifts from proxy war to honest conversation.

### Move 2: Introduce Spectrums or Subcategories

Break the binary:
- Instead of 'Metaverse or not,' map a **spectrum** (centralized вҶ” decentralized)
- Instead of 'DeFi or not,' create **subcategories** (lending, DEX, derivatives, etc.)

This shifts from winner-take-all definition fight to collaborative topology mapping.

## The Better Response: Keep Creating

But honestly? Most scissor label debates aren't worth engaging.

**The real opportunity:** The label emerged because something genuinely interesting is happening.

- Hyperpop: There was real musical innovation underneath the label fight
- Metaverse: There are real experiments in persistent virtual worlds
- Web3: There are real decentralization experiments

## The SOPHIE Principle

SOPHIE (pioneering Hyperpop artist) didn't argue about definitions. She **kept creating**, pushing the sound forward.

Her work **shaped** the category through creation, not debate.

## Influence Through Creation

Categories are **dynamic**. They're shaped by those who keep creating:
- Don't debate whether your work fits the label
- Create work so compelling it **influences** the label
- Let the category adapt to you, not vice versa

## The Ultimate Reframe

Scissor label debates anchor you to the **current moment**.

Future orientation means:
- Find the interesting idea underneath the label
- Build on it
- Move it forward
- Shape what comes next

Win by creating the future, not by debating the present.
key_ideas: Move 1: Surface real stakes beyond binary definition question
Move 2: Break binary with spectrums or subcategories
Better response: Find interesting idea, keep creating forward
SOPHIE principle: Influence category through work, not debate
Categories are dynamic; creators shape them by building, not arguing
lessons.exercise:
Scissor Label л…јмҹҒмқҙ л°ңмғқн–Ҳмқ„ л•Ң к°ҖмһҘ мғқмӮ°м Ғмқё лҢҖмқ‘мқҖ?

1) Find the interesting idea underneath and keep creating to influence the category
2) л…јмҹҒмқҳ м–‘мёЎ мһ…мһҘмқ„ л¶„м„қн•ҳм—¬ к°қкҙҖм Ғмқҙкі  нҸ¬кҙ„м Ғмқё м •мқҳлҘј м ңм•Ҳн•ҳкі  н•©мқҳлҘј лҸ„м¶ң
3) н•ҙлӢ№ л Ҳмқҙлё”мқҳ мӮ¬мҡ©мқ„ мҷ„м „нһҲ мӨ‘лӢЁн•ҳкі  мғҲлЎңмҡҙ мҡ©м–ҙлҘј л§Ңл“Өм–ҙ нҳјлһҖмқ„ нҡҢн”ј
4) м •мқҳ л…јмҹҒ мһҗмІҙм—җ м Ғк·№ м°ём—¬н•ҳм—¬ мһҗмӢ мқҳ н”„лЎңм қнҠём—җ мң лҰ¬н•ң л°©н–ҘмңјлЎң л Ҳмқҙлё”мқ„ м„ м җ
lessons.answer: Find the interesting idea underneath and keep creating to influence the category
lessons.explanation: Scissor labels emerge when something genuinely interesting appears. The debate is a distraction. SOPHIE didn't argue about Hyperpop definitions вҖ” she created groundbreaking music that shaped what the category became. In Web3, the builders creating compelling decentralized apps matter more than those arguing what 'counts' as Web3. Categories are dynamic; creators define them by building, not debating.
